sub_questions.question: Who originally claimed the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels?
sub_questions.answer: The core assertion originates from a fictional scenario analysis titled "Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained" published by Muflil Hidayat on discoveryalert.com.au. Within this hypothetical future, the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) is described as having announced the closure.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: The 2026 Hormuz closure was triggered by military conflict involving Iran, the United States, and Israel, with hostilities escalating from late February 2026.

sub_questions.question: What specifically does 'effectively closed' mean in the context of the claim?
sub_questions.answer: In the fictional 2026 scenario, 'effectively closed' involves Iran issuing warnings, forbidding passage, boarding and attacking merchant ships, laying sea mines, and advancing a legislative bill for a permanent ban on US/Israel-linked vessels. It also details significant disruptions to shipping traffic, with volumes falling to near zero, and a subsequent US counter-blockade of Iranian ports.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) issued warnings forbidding passage through the strait, boarded and attacked merchant ships, and laid sea mines.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: By the ninth week of the blockade, traffic through the strait had fallen to near zero, an operational halt with no direct modern precedent.

sub_questions.question: When was this claim originally made or reported by a primary source?
sub_questions.answer: The fictional event described in the claim is set to begin in late February 2026. The report detailing this hypothetical scenario was published on April 27, 2026, by Muflil Hidayat on discoveryalert.com.au, presenting a future event as if it has already occurred.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: ON APRIL 27, 2026...That moment arrived in early 2026, and the Strait of Hormuz oil supply disruption is now cascading through energy markets...

sub_questions.question: Where was this claim first reported by a reputable source, and what was the original language if applicable?
sub_questions.answer: The claim originates from the article "Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained" on discoveryalert.com.au, which presents a hypothetical future scenario in English. This website specializes in analysis for the ASX mining and energy sectors and sometimes publishes speculative or 'what if' scenarios.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained
sources.quote: Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained

sources.title: Discoveryalert.com.au Review
sources.quote: Discovery Alert appears to be a legitimate website focused on providing fast, AI-powered news alerts and analysis for the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX) mining and energy sectors.

sub_questions.question: Is there any official statement or directive from a relevant authority (e.g., Iran, US, Israel, international maritime organizations) confirming or denying this closure?
sub_questions.answer: There are no official statements or directives from real-world authorities (Iran, US, Israel, or international maritime organizations) confirming a current closure of the Strait of Hormuz to US/Israel-linked vessels. The IRGC's announcement is part of the fictional 2026 scenario, not a current event.

attribution_chain.description: Tweet by @currentreport1 claiming "Strait of Hormuz effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels"
attribution_chain.url: N/A - initial claim from user prompt

attribution_chain.description: Various news aggregators and AI summaries referencing a "2026 conflict scenario" or "2026 Iran war", treating it as a current event.
Article "Strait of Hormuz Oil Supply Disruption: 2026 Crisis Explained" by Muflil Hidayat on discoveryalert.com.au, presenting a hypothetical future scenario as a news report from April 27, 2026.

supporting_evidence.quote: The 2026 Hormuz closure was triggered by military conflict involving Iran, the United States, and Israel, with hostilities escalating from late February 2026.
supporting_evidence.relevance: This article is the source of the hypothetical scenario that the claim is based upon.

supporting_evidence.quote: Discovery Alert appears to be a legitimate website focused on providing fast, AI-powered news alerts and analysis for the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX) mining and energy sectors.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Confirms the nature of discoveryalert.com.au as a legitimate but analytical/speculative site, not a pure news outlet reporting current events.

contradicting_evidence.quote: Search results for "Strait of Hormuz current maritime activity" show recent articles (e.g., from aa.com.tr, timesofisrael.com, windward.ai) that, despite recent publication dates, describe events occurring in "2026" within a hypothetical conflict scenario. There is no corroborating evidence from major international news agencies or official government/maritime sources indicating a current closure or significant disruption to US/Israel-linked vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.
contradicting_evidence.relevance: The absence of real-world, current reporting from established news organizations and official bodies refutes the claim as a present-day event. The articles that do appear are referring to a future, hypothetical scenario.

summary: The claim that the "Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels" is refuted as a current event. The claim originates from a hypothetical future conflict scenario, specifically one set in 2026, which has been presented in a narrative format as a future news report on a specialized analysis website (discoveryalert.com.au). While this website provides analysis, its content on this specific topic is a speculative "what if" scenario, not a factual report of present-day events. There is no evidence from official sources or mainstream news outlets confirming a current closure or significant disruption related to US/Israel-linked vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.
key_finding: The claim is based on a fictional, hypothetical conflict scenario set in 2026, not a current real-world event.
